node_2: detect objects in nearly unbounded concept pool
edge: OVD is a task to detect objects in a nearly unbounded concept pool
The distillation-based approach is a typical solution to OVD, where the knowledge of rich and unseen categories is distilled from pre-trained vision-language models (VLMs).
The distillation-based approach involves distilling knowledge from pre-trained VLMs to address the task of Open-vocabulary object detection.
Existing distillation-based object detectors align individual region embeddings to features extracted by the frozen image encoder of VLMs, using carefully designed strategies.
VLMs learn aligned image and text representations on large-scale image-text pairs to distill general knowledge that is beneficial for Open-vocabulary object detection.

VLMs have implicitly learned the inherent co-existence of stuff and things from a colossal amount of image-text pairs.
MaskCLIP leverages the notion for zero-shot segmentation.
Existing distillation-based OVD approaches have yet to fully exploit the compositional structures encapsulated in VLMs.
Explicitly learning the co-existence of visual concepts encourages the model to understand the scene beyond just recognizing isolated individual objects.

node_2: co-occurrence of a bag of visual concepts
edge: We explore a neighborhood sampling strategy that samples boxes around region proposals to help model the co-occurrence of a bag of visual concepts.
BARON obtains the bag-of-regions embeddings by projecting the regional features into the word embedding space and encoding these pseudo words with the text encoder (TE) of a frozen VLM.
By projecting region features to pseudo words, BARON naturally allows TE to effectively represent the co-occurring semantic concepts and understand the whole scene.
BARON projects box shape and box center position into embeddings and adds to pseudo words before feeding them to TE. BARON is trained by aligning bag-of-regions embeddings to teacher embeddings.
BARON is aligned with VLM in the training process. Consistent with VLMs' pre-training, BARON uses contrastive learning approach to learn pseudo words and bag-of-regions embeddings.
Combined with Faster R-CNN, BARON achieves performance improvements in box AP50 and mask mAP on OV-COCO and OV-LVIS.
BARON can distill knowledge from caption supervision, outperforming previous approaches that use COCO caption.
The teacher embeddings are obtained by feeding image crops enclosing the bag of regions to the image encoder (IE) of the VLM.

BARON attempts to lift the learning from individual regions to the bag.

BARON is the first attempt to lift the learning from individual regions to the bag of regions for OVD. BARON is general that it can align the bag-of-regions embeddings to not only image representations but also text representations.

BARON is based on a Faster R-CNN whose classifier is replaced by a linear layer to map region features into pseudo words. BARON takes region proposals and its surrounding boxes to form bags of regions. BARON obtains student and teacher embeddings for the bag of regions from the pre-trained VLMs. BARON learns the alignment by the InfoNCE loss and maintains queues of embeddings to provide sufficient negative pairs for InfoNCE loss.
The bag of regions is treated as a bag of words to obtain the bag-of-regions embedding.
The bag of regions from the region proposals are aligned with the corresponding features from VLMs.
BARON learns the alignment by the InfoNCE loss and maintains queues of embeddings to provide sufficient negative pairs for InfoNCE loss.
The idea can also be used for other architectures applicable for OVD.
Faster R-CNN is used to detect objects from arbitrary vocabularies by replacing the original classifier with a linear layer that projects region features into the word embedding space.
The linear layer maps region features to multiple pseudo words to represent the rich semantic information of each object.
Pseudo words are fed into the text encoder to calculate the similarity with category embeddings for obtaining final classification results.

Human language has the ability to generalize concepts, allowing learned concepts to be applied to describe or recognize new things.
The paper explores the ability of large-scale VLMs to represent the compositional structure of multiple concepts through grouping contextually related regions into a bag.
A contrastive learning approach is used to align the bag-of-regions representations of the detector and pre-trained VLMs, achieving state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks.
The compositional structure explored in the paper focuses on the co-occurrence of objects, which behaves similar to a bag of words.
The goal is to endow VLMs with human-like compositional representation to achieve more generalized intelligence.
